impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the defense that the tied products are 'nonstaple goods' lacking substantial non-infringing uses shields the patent holder from antitrust liability.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying by conditioning licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' exclusive purchase of Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court held that whether honeycomb products had substantial non-infringing uses was a jury question and upheld the damages award. This reinforces that patent holders cannot leverage patent rights to foreclose competition in separate product markets.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies accused of patent infringement by competitors engaging in tying arrangements can pursue antitrust counterclaims and recover damages, providing a powerful defensive tool against anticompetitive patent licensing practices.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who condition licenses or supply agreements on exclusive purchase of related but unpatented products face significant antitrust liability risk, even where the tied products are used in patented systems.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in patent prosecution and on a Walker Process fraud claim alleging attempted monopolization through enforcement of a fraudulently obtained patent.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct and Walker Process antitrust fraud claims related to patents on coiled tubing products, finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. The court held that factual questions remained about whether the patentee intentionally withheld material prior art (CYMAX Documents) during prosecution and whether that conduct amounted to fraud sufficient for attempted monopolization liability. The case was remanded for trial.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors facing enforcement of potentially fraudulently obtained patents have a viable path to Walker Process antitrust claims where evidence of intentional withholding of material prior art exists.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who acquire prior art through asset purchases face heightened scrutiny of their disclosure obligations during prosecution, with potential exposure to both unenforceability and antitrust liability.

impacts.caseName: REGENXBIO Inc. v. Sarepta Therapeutics, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether claims directed to cultured host cells containing a recombinant nucleic acid molecule encoding an AAV capsid protein and a heterologous non-AAV sequence are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101 or directed to a natural phenomenon.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's grant of summary judgment of patent ineligibility, holding that claims to genetically engineered host cells containing recombinant nucleic acid molecules with specific AAV capsid sequences and heterologous non-AAV sequences are not directed to a natural phenomenon. This is a significant ruling for the gene therapy and biotechnology industries, broadening patent eligibility for engineered biological constructs used in therapeutic development.
impacts.positiveImplications: Biotech and gene therapy companies gain stronger patent protection for engineered biological products, supporting investment in gene therapy platforms and reinforcing the patentability of man-made biological constructs.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies developing competing gene therapy vectors may face broader patent infringement exposure for engineered host cells and viral vector constructs.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming methods and systems for a server that tailors content presentation specifications to a requesting wireless device's screen size and capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's patents claiming server-side tailoring of content delivery specifications to wireless device capabilities were ineligible under § 101, reversing the jury's infringement verdict for GoTV on the '715 patent and entering judgment for Netflix. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of tailoring content to a device, a concept long practiced in the art. This decision narrows patent protection for content delivery and adaptive streaming technologies.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies like Netflix gain protection from patent assertions covering fundamental concepts of adaptive content delivery and device-specific content formatting.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the streaming and content delivery space face increased risk that claims directed to server-side content optimization will be invalidated as abstract ideas under § 101.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Board governing discretionary denials of inter partes review institution are 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions governing discretionary IPR non-institution decisions are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking. The court emphasized that Congress gave the Director unreviewable discretion over institution, the instructions do not bind the Director, and they merely guide the Board's exercise of delegated authority. This preserves the PTO's flexibility to issue and modify IPR institution guidance without formal rulemaking.
impacts.positiveImplications: The PTO retains broad flexibility to adjust IPR institution policies quickly, which may benefit patent holders who prefer fewer IPR challenges to their patents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies that rely heavily on IPR to challenge patents cannot use the APA to compel more liberal institution practices, potentially limiting their ability to use IPR as a defense against patent assertions.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses in countervailing duty and antidumping investigations as confidential is consistent with 19 U.S.C. §§ 1516a and 1677f, and whether section 1516a abrogates the common law right of public access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of International Trade's holding that the ITC's blanket practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with the governing statute and that section 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial records. This decision requires the ITC to make individualized confidentiality determinations rather than treating all submissions as confidential by default.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers and domestic industries gain greater transparency in trade remedy proceedings, potentially enabling better access to information for litigation and compliance purposes.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies submitting commercially sensitive data in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations face increased risk of public disclosure, as automatic confidential treatment is no longer available.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent holder's damages expert report and granting summary judgment for absence of a remedy based on that exclusion.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report in a patent infringement case against Microsoft and vacated the resulting summary judgment. The court found the district court improperly excluded the expert testimony, which was the sole basis for granting summary judgment on the ground that Exafer could not prove a remedy. This decision reinforces that courts must carefully apply Daubert standards before using expert exclusion to dispose of patent cases.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from stronger appellate scrutiny of district court decisions to exclude damages experts, preserving their ability to present damages theories to juries.
impacts.negativeImplications: Accused infringers face reduced ability to use Daubert motions as a pathway to summary judgment by eliminating damages evidence.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether OPM's regulatory writing requirement for overtime authorization in 5 C.F.R. § 550.111(c) is a valid exercise of rulemaking authority under the statutory requirement in 5 U.S.C. § 5542(a) that the government pay for overtime 'officially ordered or approved.'
impacts.description: In an en banc decision, the Federal Circuit upheld OPM's longstanding regulation requiring written authorization for overtime pay for federal employees, affirming dismissal of claims by IHS nurses who worked significant uncompensated overtime during the COVID-19 pandemic. The 6-4 decision validated the 80-year-old regulatory requirement. However, a separate panel decision reversed dismissal of related claims for nighttime, Sunday, holiday, and miscellaneous compensation enhancements under Title 38.
impacts.positiveImplications: Federal agencies benefit from the validation of the written overtime authorization requirement, providing clear administrative control over overtime obligations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal employees, particularly healthcare workers, face a significant barrier to recovering overtime pay when supervisors are aware of but do not formally authorize overtime work in writing, even during emergencies.

impacts.caseName: Game Plan, Inc. v. Uninterrupted IP, LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether common law trademark rights acquired through an asset purchase agreement can establish priority over a prior federal registration in a cancellation proceeding before the TTAB under Section 2(d) of the Lanham Act.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the TTAB's cancellation of Game Plan's trademark registration for 'I AM MORE THAN AN ATHLETE' and dismissal of Game Plan's opposition to UNIP's mark applications. UNIP established priority through common law rights acquired via an asset purchase agreement that transferred both the mark and associated goodwill. The decision confirms that acquired common law rights, when properly assigned with goodwill, can defeat a federal registration in TTAB proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses can strategically acquire common law trademark rights through asset purchases to establish priority and challenge competing registrations, providing flexibility in brand portfolio management.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal trademark registrants face the risk that a party acquiring common law rights through post-registration assignments can successfully cancel their registrations if the acquired rights predate the registration.

impacts.caseName: Crocs, Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC properly issued only a limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents under 19 U.S.C. § 1337(g)(1) rather than a general exclusion order, and whether the Commission's finding of no trademark violation against active respondents was timely appealed.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit dismissed Crocs's appeal of the ITC's no-violation finding against active respondents as untimely and affirmed the limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents. The court held that § 1337(g)(1) mandates issuance of an LEO against defaulting parties upon request, presuming complaint allegations true, and the ITC did not abuse its discretion in declining to issue a broader general exclusion order. This clarifies the scope of relief available in ITC trademark investigations against defaulting and active respondents.
impacts.positiveImplications: Brand owners can rely on the ITC's mandatory LEO provisions against defaulting respondents as a backstop against counterfeit imports, even when claims against active respondents fail.
impacts.negativeImplications: Brand owners seeking broad general exclusion orders face significant hurdles when active respondents defeat infringement claims, as relief is limited to defaulting parties only.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Restaurant Revitalization Fund statute creates a mandatory entitlement to grant payments that is money-mandating for Tucker Act jurisdiction, and whether the SBA violated the statute by awarding grants out of the statutorily required order.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that over 300 restaurants stated viable claims against the United States for unpaid RRF grants, holding that the statute's use of 'shall award grants' and 'shall' be 'equal to' the pandemic-related revenue loss created a mandatory obligation. The court found the SBA violated the statute by processing and awarding grants out of the order in which applications were received, giving later applicants priority over earlier ones. This decision opens the door to significant government liability for misadministered pandemic relief programs.
impacts.positiveImplications: Restaurants and businesses denied pandemic relief grants due to agency processing errors have a viable damages claim against the government, potentially recovering the full grant amounts they were statutorily entitled to receive.
impacts.negativeImplications: The government faces potential liability running into billions of dollars for misadministration of mandatory grant programs where Congress used mandatory statutory language.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTAB correctly construed claim terms in a patent directed to streaming partly encrypted media content, and whether the Board's construction supported its rejection of an obviousness challenge.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the PTAB's claim construction in an IPR challenging DivX's patent on streaming encrypted media content, vacated the Board's decision upholding patentability, and remanded. The court adopted a different claim construction that could affect the obviousness analysis. This decision is significant for the streaming and digital rights management industries, potentially narrowing patent protection for encrypted content delivery systems.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming platforms benefit from the reversal of a claim construction that could have sustained a broad patent on encrypted media streaming, potentially reducing licensing costs.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the DRM and encrypted streaming space face risk that narrow claim constructions will render their patents more vulnerable to obviousness challenges in IPR proceedings.

impacts.caseName: Micron Technology Inc. v. Longhorn IP LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Circuit has appellate jurisdiction over an interlocutory order imposing an $8 million bond under the Idaho Bad Faith Assertions of Patent Infringement Act, and whether the Act is preempted by federal patent law.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit dismissed appeals from an $8 million bond order imposed under Idaho's Bad Faith Assertions of Patent Infringement Act for lack of jurisdiction over the interlocutory order. While the court did not reach the merits of federal preemption, the case highlights the growing use of state bad-faith patent assertion statutes against patent assertion entities. The bond requirement remains in effect pending further proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses targeted by potentially abusive patent assertions can leverage state bad-faith patent assertion statutes to impose significant bond requirements on asserting entities, creating a powerful deterrent.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent assertion entities face potentially crippling bond requirements under state laws that may effectively prevent them from pursuing patent enforcement, and appellate review of such orders may be deferred.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding expert testimony on induced patent infringement and erred in granting judgment as a matter of law to the accused infringer.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of two expert witnesses and its grant of JMOL to DePuy in a patent infringement case involving spinal surgery derotation tools. The court held the district court abused its discretion in excluding the experts and remanded for a new trial. This decision is significant for medical device patent litigation, reinforcing that expert testimony on how surgical devices are used in clinical practice should not be lightly excluded.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the medical device space benefit from appellate protection against overly aggressive exclusion of expert testimony regarding clinical use of accused devices.
impacts.negativeImplications: Medical device companies face renewed infringement exposure when appellate courts reverse expert exclusion rulings that were the basis for defense judgments.

impacts.caseName: Wonderland Switzerland AG v. Evenflo Company, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly upheld jury verdicts of patent infringement under the doctrine of equivalents for car seat patents and properly entered a permanent injunction against the infringer.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit largely affirmed jury verdicts finding Evenflo's car seats infringe Wonderland's patents on child car seat designs, including under the doctrine of equivalents, while modifying aspects of the permanent injunction. The court also addressed whether denial of a new trial on willful infringement was proper. This decision has significant implications for the competitive children's products market and the scope of injunctive relief in design patent cases.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the consumer products space benefit from affirmation of doctrine-of-equivalents verdicts and availability of permanent injunctions against competitors.
impacts.negativeImplications: Consumer product manufacturers face significant risk from broad equivalents-based infringement findings that extend patent protection beyond literal claim scope, backed by injunctive relief.
